title: Feral Cats in Spain: Feline Colonies and the TNR Method 🐱
description: There are over a million feral cats in Spain living in feline colonies. The TNR (Trap, Neuter, Return) method is the only ethical and effective solution according to science. Discover how colonies work, who the managers are, and what Spanish law says about feral cats.

segments.voice: In Spain, there are over a million cats living on the streets. They form colonies, breed uncontrollably, and many die before the age of one. But in cities like Barcelona, Madrid, and Valencia, a silent army of volunteers is changing their fate with science and compassion. It's called the TNR method, and it works.

Welcome to CatBrothers. Today, we're going to talk about the cats that don't have a home. Feline colonies are a reality in all Spanish cities, and the way we manage them has enormous consequences for the cats, wildlife, and public health. This documentary will change your perspective.
segments.visual: group of stray cats in alley

segments.voice: A feline colony is a stable group of cats that share a territory and resources. According to the National Association of Animal Friends, there are over five hundred thousand registered colonies in Spain. They can have from three to over a hundred cats. They form around food sources: restaurants, containers, parks, and markets.
segments.visual: feral cats eating together outdoor colony

segments.voice: The reproductive biology of cats explains why colonies grow so quickly. A female cat can have her first litter at five months of age. She can have between two and three litters a year, with four to six kittens each. Dr. Julie Levy from the University of Florida calculated that a pair of cats can theoretically produce four hundred and twenty thousand descendants in seven years without control.
segments.visual: mother cat with many kittens outside

segments.voice: The TNR method means Trap, Neuter, and Return. It involves capturing the cats in a colony with humane traps, surgically neutering them, marking them with a cut in the left ear, and returning them to their territory. A study by the University of Florida published in the Journal of the American Veterinary Medical Association showed that TNR reduces colonies by sixty to eighty percent in six years.

Why return the cats to the street instead of taking them to a shelter? Because feral adult cats are not socializable. Dr. Sharon Crowell-Davis from the University of Georgia established that cats not socialized with humans before eight weeks of age rarely adapt to a home. Keeping them in shelter cages is cruelty, not protection.
segments.visual: feral cat ear tipped returned colony

segments.voice: Additionally, there's the vacuum effect. When all the cats are removed from an area, other cats quickly migrate to occupy the territory and available resources. A study by the University of Rome published in Animal Welfare documented that areas where entire colonies were eliminated had the same number of cats again in less than two years.
segments.visual: new stray cat arriving in empty territory

segments.voice: The Animal Rights and Welfare Law approved in Spain in 2023 legally recognizes feline colonies and establishes the TNR method as mandatory management. Municipalities must register colonies, authorize managers, and facilitate neutering. Eliminating or relocating colonies without authorization is now an administrative offense.

Colony managers are the silent heroes of this story. They are volunteers, mostly women, who feed, monitor, and coordinate neutering in the colonies. According to data from the Federation of Animal Protection and Defense Associations, over eighty-five percent of colony managers in Spain are women over forty-five years old.
segments.visual: volunteer woman feeding stray cats colony

segments.voice: Barcelona was a pioneer in Spain with its municipal TNR program started in 2001. The city went from over nine thousand stray cats to approximately five thousand five hundred in a decade, according to data from the Barcelona City Council. The program includes over a thousand controlled feeding points and a team of municipal veterinarians dedicated exclusively to neutering.

Madrid implemented its feline colony management protocol in 2017. Through Madrid Salud, the city council registered over five hundred colonies and established agreements with animal protection organizations to perform neutering. In the first three years, over eight thousand cats were neutered, according to data from the Department of Environmental Health of the Madrid City Council.

The cost of neutering ranges from fifty to one hundred euros per cat, according to the Spanish Association of Small Animal Veterinary Specialists. It seems expensive, but a study by the Autonomous University of Barcelona calculated that keeping a cat in a shelter costs over one thousand two hundred euros per year. TNR is the most economical option for municipalities in the medium term.

Controlled feeding is a fundamental part of TNR. Managers feed at fixed times in designated points with suitable food. This allows for census taking, detection of unneutered cats, identification of sick cats, and reduction of food searching in containers. A study by the University of Seville confirmed that controlled feeding reduces neighborhood conflicts by forty percent.

Kittens born in colonies have a socialization window. If they are captured before eight weeks and socialized with humans, they can be adopted as normal domestic cats. Animal protection organizations prioritize the early capture of kittens. A study by the University of Bristol showed that kittens socialized before seven weeks have a ninety-two percent success rate in adoption.
segments.visual: tiny kitten being held gently rescued

segments.voice: The health of feral cats is better than many believe. A study by the Complutense University of Madrid analyzed three hundred cats from managed colonies and found that eighty percent were in normal body condition, and only three percent had serious diseases. Well-managed colonies produce reasonably healthy cats.

However, life expectancy differs dramatically. An indoor cat lives from twelve to eighteen years. A managed colony cat lives from four to eight years. An unmanaged feral cat lives from two to four years. Data from the University of Florida shows that TNR with controlled feeding almost doubles life expectancy compared to unmanaged colonies.

The impact on wildlife is a controversial topic. A 2013 study published in Nature Communications estimated that cats in the United States kill between 1.3 and 4 billion birds annually. However, researchers from the University of Exeter argued in 2020 that in urban environments, cats primarily hunt rodents, and their impact on birds is significantly lower.
segments.visual: cat watching birds from distance urban

segments.voice: Alternatives to TNR do not work. Mass killing was tried for decades without success. A meta-analysis by the University of Central Florida reviewed forty-two studies and concluded that no mass killing program permanently reduced feral cat populations. Elimination is ineffective, costly, and ethically questionable.

Technology is improving TNR. In Australia, the RSPCA uses GPS microchips to track colony movements. In Spain, several animal protection organizations are testing mobile applications for managers to record censuses, neutering, and health status of each cat. The Colony Cat Care platform already operates in over fifty European cities.
segments.visual: cat with ear tip microchip technology

segments.voice: The legal aspect is still evolving. Before the 2023 law, poisoning or illegally trapping feral cats was common. The new legislation classifies animal mistreatment as a criminal offense with penalties of up to eighteen months in prison. Registered feline colonies have legal protection, and authorized managers have the right to access feeding points.

Neighborhood conflicts are the biggest obstacle. Some residents complain about noise, smell, and excrement. A pilot program by the University of Valencia demonstrated that community information sessions reduce complaints by sixty-five percent. When neighbors understand that TNR reduces the population and controlled feeding minimizes problems, opposition decreases.

More and more Spanish municipalities are investing in TNR as public policy. Data from the Affinity Foundation shows that ninety-two percent of municipalities with over fifty thousand inhabitants already have feline colony management protocols. Spain is at the European forefront in feline colony protection legislation.

If you live near a feline colony, you can help. Contact the local animal protection organization to learn about the TNR program in your area. You can be a volunteer manager, donate for neutering, or simply spread correct information. Each neutered cat prevents hundreds of unwanted births and premature deaths on the street.
segments.visual: volunteer caring for stray cats kindly

segments.voice: Feral cats did not choose the street. They are the result of abandonment, irresponsible breeding, and lack of neutering. TNR gives them dignity: a free but controlled life, without the suffering of uncontrolled reproduction. Science, law, and compassion are finally on the same side.
segments.visual: stray cat looking at camera hopeful eyes

segments.voice: If this documentary moved you, share it and subscribe to CatBrothers now. Activate the bell because what's coming next week will fascinate you: the whiskers of a cat are a more advanced sensory organ than you ever imagined. The science behind those hairs is striking. Don't miss it.
segments.visual: happy cat waving paw at camera
